speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of myself, Maurice Rabbie Raynor, in a pram with my late sister, Helen Rabbie. My mother is the youngest of the two adults. My mother, Cissie Rabbe Rabbie, and her mother, Rebecca Rabbe Klock, who came to England to visit us in Holland where this picture was taken in Amsterdam, approximately 1934. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This photograph was taken in Amsterdam, I would say approximately 1935, maybe 1936. As you look at the photograph, I, Maurice Rabbie Raynor, on the left with the crossed legs. Next to me holding my left leg is my late sister, Helen Rabbie. Behind me, the tall girl is the daughter of my uncle, Sam Rabbe, the brother of my father, Isaac Rabbie. Her name was Leni Rabbe. And to her left, the small boy, his name is Segfried Fontaine, the son, my cousin, of my father's late sister, Henrietta yet Fontaine Rabbie. I, Maurice, I am the only survivor of that group from the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph taken of my mother, Cissie Rabbe Rabbie, and my late father, Isaac Rabbie. This was taken before I was born, so therefore it's before 1932, as I, Maurice Rabbie Raynor, was born in 1932. This possibly was taken on the occasion of their engagement. I'm not sure where it was taken. It could have been taken in England, as my mother was born originally in England. My father born in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in the garden of my Aunt [? Rae, ?] the sister of my mother's mother. It was taken in Ilford in the suburbs of London. Together with me is my second cousin, Mickey Italeander. And was-- this photograph was taken before the war, approximately 1938-39 in England. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is the first passport that was issued to me after World War II. This is a Dutch passport issued to me a year after the liberation in 1946. This photograph was taken of me in England as the passport was issued by the Dutch consulate in London. And this was a photograph of me in 1946. The small insignia on my left lapel is an insignia of the Boy Scouts, to which I belonged at that time when I was in England after the liberation. I was 14 years old. And it is me, Maurice Rabbie Raynor, of course. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph taken by a photographer of the British army in the barrack where my sister and I, where the diamond transport children were. This is a photograph of my late sister, Helen Rabbie, after the 15th of April, 1945, and the liberation. She died on the 24th of April, 1945, in the hospital outside the Belsen concentration camp at age 12 years old. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Starting as you look at the photograph from left to right. My mother Cissie Rabbe Rabbie, next to her, my late father Isaac Rabbie. Next to my father, my Aunt Mari Rabbe, married to my late Uncle Sam Rabbe, the eldest brother of my mother. Next to her, Ann Fransman Rabbie, my mother's late sister. Next to her, the child, a little child being held up is my late sister, Helen Rabbie. She's being held up by my mother's other sister, Lili Rabbe. Next to her is a small young lady whose name is Betty Firsch, the sister of my Aunt Mari Rabbe. Next to her, not behind her, next to her is my late Uncle Sam Rabbe, my mother's eldest brother, husband of Mari Rabbe. And next to him, his late father-in-law, Mr. Firsch. On the front row, the small boy is Maurice Rabbie Raynor, that is myself. And holding my hand, the little bigger boy is my cousin, Mickey Italeander. The photograph was taken in England. I would say I was about three years old, so therefore in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maurice, would you like to tell us how you found these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. These pictures were all given by my mother when-- after the occupation of Holland and the Jewish people started to be deported-- my mother gave all these photographs to my school friend Jopi's parents, for safe keeping, also with other things from the family, some valuable, some not valuable. And not valuable, that means in the same way. And these were all given to that family. And we got all these photographs back when my mother and I came back from the camps after the liberation. That is how we have these photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph taken in Amsterdam. This was taken after the liberation in the year 1947. On the right with the dark hair, myself Maurice Rabbie Raynor. And on my right, my schoolfriend, non-Jewish schoolfriend, my late friend, Jopi Nipot. He was my schoolfriend [SOBBING] all the years that I lived there. His parents were friends of my parents. It is his parents that kept these photographs. Unfortunately he passed away about three years ago of an incurable disease. He was four months younger than myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my only child, my daughter of my first marriage. Her name is Helen, after my late sister, Helen Isabel, in actual fact, Rabbie Raynor. That is her proper name. She is now known by the last name of Jackson, because her, her stepfather's name is Jackson. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This photograph is of myself, Maurice Rabbie Raynor, with my wife Terry Jane. It was taken in England at a civil wedding ceremony in the year [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] 1985. My wife, no doubt, will correct me. This is when I got married to my wife Terry, Terry Jane. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This photograph is taken of myself with my wife in 1995, when we returned to Belsen concentration camp, or the site of the former concentration camp, for the 50-year memorial service. We are standing on the platform, or siding, the very same platform where I and my parents and sister arrived in, in the beginning, January 1944 from the camp in Holland, Westerbork. The siding is still there today, this one being just outside the Belsen camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken April 1995 in the small clearing, which is within the confines of what is now the-- the British army base, the former German army base, which after the-- after the liberation of the camp was-- the base was turned into a hospital. And it is here that my late sister is buried. The camp is outside the small village of Bergen, which gives Belsen its proper name, Bergen-Belsen. Myself, my wife, and my cousin John's wife, Susan Fransman. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This photograph was taken in April 1995 on the railway platform in Belsen, or Bergen, outside the camp Belsen. Myself in the middle, Maurice Rabbie Raynor, my wife, Terry Jane, and on my other-- on my right side is my cousin, John Fransman, whose mother, my aunt, my mother's sister, she unfortunately recently died. And he and his mother were in the camp with me in Westerbork and in Belsen. He's six years younger than me and was liberated by the Americans with his mother outside of [? Hilersladen, ?] Germany, also, of course in 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken at the end of 1995 in Miami, Florida, in the United States. Myself, Maurice, with the lady who was very instrumental in making possible my survival, and the survival of my other children of the diamond transport in Belsen. Her name is Luba, and she lives in Miami. She's originally from Poland. And I believe she's approximately 80 years old, although ladies don't give their age easily.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in Amsterdam on the 15th of April 1995 in the liberal shul in Amsterdam south. This photograph is taken on the second night of Seder, which was exactly 50 years since Bergen-Belsen was liberated. The people you see in this photograph are some of the surviving children of the diamond transport. And the lady to whom I am pointing is Luba Frederick, the lady who was instrumental in saving our lives. Next to her is Hetty Werkendum, who lives in Australia. I, myself, I'm there with the mustache, Maurice Rabbie Raynor. This gentleman, Jerry [? Luckmucker, ?] lives in London. This lady's last name, maiden name, was [? Dahen, ?] lives in New York. This gentleman, Abraham Reichschman, or Bram Reichschman, lives just outside of Amsterdam. This is his sister. This man's last name is [? Mandach. ?] He is an artist in the Netherlands. This man's name is Max [? Klierekoper. ?] Lives in Amsterdam. These two men are related. They are called [? Dahen. ?] The man who made it all possible is this man here, Isaac Rodriguez Cohen, who lives in Santa Monica, United States. The man next to me that you can hardly see is Max Wierkendum, one of the two brothers of Hetty Werkendum. The lady behind Hetty Werkendum is Henny Pollack, who lives in Amsterdam. I think that's all I can remember at the moment. This document is a Dutch document issued in 1955 by the Dutch Red Cross. It is written in German. And it shows that I, Maurice Rabbie, born 1932. And the last place I lived was in [INAUDIBLE] Number 60 in Amsterdam. And it further goes to say that because of racial, and particularly Jewish forefathers, was deported to the Dutch camp Westerbork, and later on to the camp Belsen. And it finishes by saying that after some time in the hospital in Bergen-Belsen, I returned to Holland. I'm sharing this document as part of my testimony to make viewers of this document aware that I have been trying for many years to use the proof of where I was as a way of obtaining from the Netherlands government a pension and [NON-ENGLISH] for the years that were taken away from me as a school child in the most formative years of my life. Being born in 1932, I was eight years old when Holland capitulated. And I've tried to use this proof, as if I have to use proof, to obtain a pension from the Dutch government, for monies that the German government gave to the Dutch government for the benefit of survivors of the Holocaust. And as far as I know, the very few of us real survivors that are left, all of us who are getting something, of which I am not one, are having a terrible problem with the Dutch government of obtaining anything at all. Not alone. For the 50 years that have gone past, which they won't compensate, or for the 50 years or whatever years we still have left, we're having a very, very hard time with the Dutch bureaucracy to get [NON-ENGLISH] from them. Now I'll--
